repairBuyOrReplace.h2: C-Class C300d — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential maintenance — catch it before catastrophic failure
Piston-valve contact means little is left to repair
Standard maintenance on any modern diesel
Check sensors, wiring and dosing injector

buyingChecks.title: Buying This C300d? Check These First
buyIf: * Post-2020 OM654 M (MHEV) with improved timing chain components
* Full Mercedes service history with regular oil changes (critical for chain longevity)
* EGR cooler and DPF recently serviced
* AdBlue system functioning correctly (no warning lights)
* Evidence of regular longer runs (DPF health)
avoidIf: * Cold-start rattling and no timing chain history — assume it's overdue
* No service history — the OM654 is sensitive to oil quality
* Short-journey ownership history — DPF and EGR issues likely
* Unexplained power loss or limp mode — EGR cooler or DPF issues
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis — may indicate timing, EGR or AdBlue problems

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The C300d is Mercedes' top-spec 2.0-litre diesel in the C-Class range — a 261 bhp, 550 Nm four-cylinder that delivers exceptional performance and refinement. The OM654 is a significant improvement over the OM651 — lighter, more efficient and quieter. With proper servicing, these units regularly exceed 180,000 miles. However, early OM654 units have reported timing chain issues, EGR cooler vacuum diaphragm failures, injector seal leaks, and DPF/AdBlue system problems. With typical C300d values ranging from £25,000 (W205) to £40,000+ (W206), an engine replacement is a major financial commitment — a well-maintained post-2020 OM654 M with full service history is the safest choice.

cta.label: → Compare C300d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz C-Class C300d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or clattering (timing chain warning) — see the map above
* Service History — Including regular oil changes (critical for timing chain longevity)
* EGR Cooler — Check for any loss of power or warning lights; vacuum diaphragm failures are common
* DPF Regeneration — Check history; short journeys accelerate wear
* AdBlue System — Check for warning lights or AdBlue faults
* Injector Health — Check for smooth idle; any rough running may indicate injector seal issues
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes read; unresolved issues may indicate timing, EGR, DPF or AdBlue problems
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures

items.question: How much does a Mercedes-Benz C-Class C300d engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned C300d engine typically costs £5,000–£7,500 supply only. Fitting adds £900–£1,400 depending on the specialist. A blown engine replacement for a Mercedes can range from £9,000 to £13,000. Used engines offer the most cost-effective solution (£1,500–£3,500), while reconditioned units provide better reliability warranties. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your C-Class.

items.question: Is the Mercedes-Benz C-Class C300d engine reliable?
items.answer: Yes — the OM654 is considered more reliable than the OM651. With proper servicing, these units regularly exceed 180,000 miles. It's lighter, more efficient and quieter. However, early OM654 units have reported timing chain issues, injector seal leaks, EGR cooler vacuum diaphragm failures, and DPF/AdBlue system problems. Regular maintenance and proactive attention to common issues are essential.

items.question: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz C-Class C300d?
items.answer: The most common issues are timing chain wear on early OM654 units (rattling on cold start), EGR cooler vacuum diaphragm failure (power loss, underboost codes), injector seal leaks (oil/diesel smells, smoke), DPF clogging (short journeys accelerate wear), and AdBlue system faults (warning lights, potential no-start). The OM654 M (MHEV) variant includes a 48V starter-generator.

items.question: Is it worth replacing a C300d engine, or should I scrap the car?
items.answer: Depends on the vehicle value and condition. Against a well-kept C300d worth £25,000–£40,000+, a £6,000–£7,000 recon is 15–25% of the car's value — worth it. Against a tired example with significant body issues, the same recon may approach our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold — get a scrap valuation first. Post-2020 OM654 M (MHEV) cars command a premium.

items.question: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz C-Class C300d engine code?
items.answer: The engine code is OM654, found on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation. It's the 2.0-litre four-cylinder diesel fitted to the C300d from 2018–present. The OM654 M variant includes a 48V integrated starter-generator. Check the VIN plate or vehicle documentation to confirm which variant you have.

items.question: Can I repair the OM654 timing chain, or does the engine need replacement?
items.answer: The timing chain and tensioner must be replaced if worn. A preventative replacement costs £1,500–£3,000. A snapped chain almost always destroys the engine, requiring a £5,000–£8,500+ replacement. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately. Regular oil changes are the key to preventing timing chain issues.
